title: Cumulative Review: Repair a Meaning Map
summary: Cumulative Review: Repair a Meaning Map retrieves prior HTML skills in an original semantic incident review scenario and requires source, parsed-structure, keyboard, and content evidence.
objectives: Apply semantic landmarks, semantic heading outline, semantic text meaning, semantic time address quotes, semantic disclosure, semantic content models, semantic native first in unfamiliar content.
Produce or audit a real semantic incident review artifact without appearance-only reasoning.
Keep earlier document requirements active while verifying native behavior and valid relationships.

steps.title: predict 1: regional heat-response portal audit · review
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim preserves meaning in the changed case? Scenario: semantic incident review.
steps.why: Cumulative Review: Repair a Meaning Map keeps meaning testable instead of depending on appearance. This is checkpoint 1 in semantic incident review.

content.title: semantic incident review evidence
content.text: Choose the claim that preserves content meaning when visual styling and browser recovery are removed.

hints: Cumulative Review: Repair a Meaning Map checkpoint 1: name the content relationship before choosing markup.
Cumulative Review: Repair a Meaning Map checkpoint 1: compare source, parsed structure, and keyboard route.
Cumulative Review: Repair a Meaning Map checkpoint 1: choose the smallest native relationship that survives changed styling.

options.text: Semantic incident review case 1: header, section, article, aside, and div are interchangeable boxes once CSS makes them look identical.
Semantic incident review case 1: choose each region from its content relationship and navigation purpose, then verify the resulting landmark map.
Semantic incident review case 1: use main for every visually large panel so assistive technology finds all important boxes.

semantic incident review phase 2.3: nest subsections beneath their actual parents

options.text: Semantic incident review case 3: strong and em are merely longer spellings of bold and italic, so they can style any text without changing meaning.
Semantic incident review case 3: select inline elements from the meaning the sentence needs, and leave purely visual treatment to CSS.
Semantic incident review case 3: use strong for every heading because bold text is always more semantic.

options.text: Semantic incident review case 5: any control that opens hidden content should be implemented with details, including navigation and modal dialogs.
Semantic incident review case 5: choose details and summary only when a native disclosure matches the information task and expected focus behavior.
Semantic incident review case 5: build every disclosure from a clickable div so the native marker never appears.

options.text: Semantic incident review case 6: if invalid nesting produces the intended pixels, the parent-child relationship has no user or interoperability cost.
Semantic incident review case 6: check the element content model, validate the source, and compare the parsed DOM before accepting the nesting.
Semantic incident review case 6: add role presentation to invalid children so the parser keeps them in place.

options.text: Semantic incident review case 11: address is the correct wrapper for any street location, even when it is not contact information for the page or article.
Semantic incident review case 11: use time, address, quotation, and citation elements only when their defined relationship matches the content.
Semantic incident review case 11: put every date in a generic span because machines infer dates from visible punctuation.
